content.text: A DPIA template provides the mandatory scaffolding that ensures every Data Protection Impact Assessment for an agentic AI feature covers the sections required by GDPR Article 35(7) and produces outputs the DPO can act on. Beyond the regulatory minimum, an agentic AI DPIA template must contain supplementary sections that capture the unique risk vectors of autonomous systems — data flows initiated by tool calls rather than user input, context memory that persists across sessions, inferences generated from behaviour patterns, and outputs that propagate across multi-agent chains. The risk mitigation plan attached to each DPIA converts the risk register into an actionable set of commitments, each with an owner, deadline, and verification mechanism.
What It Is

A DPIA template is a structured document that guides assessors through every section required for a legally valid Data Protection Impact Assessment. GDPR Article 35(7) mandates four elements in every DPIA: a systematic description of the processing and its purposes including the legitimate interests pursued; an assessment of necessity and proportionality relative to the purposes; an assessment of the risks to the rights and freedoms of data subjects; and the measures envisaged to address those risks, including safeguards and security measures. The template operationalises these four elements into answerable sections with specific prompts and evidence requirements, rather than leaving assessors to interpret the legislation directly.

For agentic AI, the template adds five supplementary sections not present in standard DPIA frameworks: an agentic data flow map that traces every data access the agent makes through its tool call graph; a memory and context analysis that assesses the scope and duration of persistent state; an inference and profiling risk analysis that evaluates what the agent can infer from behaviour patterns beyond what data subjects explicitly provide; an orchestration boundary analysis for multi-agent deployments; and a model supplier assessment that evaluates the privacy obligations created by using a third-party LLM provider.

The risk mitigation plan is a separate but closely linked document that converts each identified risk into a concrete engineering or organisational commitment. For each risk in the DPIA's risk register, the mitigation plan specifies the control measure, its type (technical or organisational), the owner, the implementation deadline, the evidence that will demonstrate completion, and the verification process. The mitigation plan is reviewed by the DPO alongside the DPIA; a DPIA cannot receive a positive DPO opinion until the mitigation plan addresses all risks rated above the acceptable threshold.
Why It Matters

Without a template, DPIA completeness is person-dependent. Assessors with legal backgrounds may adequately cover necessity and proportionality but miss the technical depth needed to identify inference risks from memory embeddings. Engineers may produce thorough technical descriptions but miss the data subject rights assessment. A template that prescribes all required sections with concrete prompts for each creates a consistent baseline regardless of the assessor's background, making DPO review faster and more predictable.

For agentic AI, the value of a template is multiplied by the novelty of the risk surface. Existing DPIA guidance pre-dates widespread agentic AI deployment; most assessors who have completed DPIAs for conventional data processing systems have no reference point for evaluating the risk that an agent's context window contains inferred health status information, or that multi-agent output chaining can amplify a single data access point across a pipeline with multiple processing purposes. The agentic supplement sections of the template establish a minimum vocabulary and set of questions that must be answered, even by assessors encountering these risks for the first time.
Benefits
Regulatory defensibility:

A completed template demonstrates systematic compliance with Article 35(7). If a supervisory authority audits the DPIA, a structured template with evidence against each mandatory section is far stronger than a narrative document of uncertain completeness.
DPO efficiency:

A consistent template allows the DPO to review DPIAs quickly because the structure is predictable — the DPO knows exactly where to look for the risk matrix, the mitigation plan, and the residual risk assessment without reading the document sequentially.
Engineering integration:

The agentic data flow map section of the template forces the engineering team to document the agent's complete tool access graph before the DPIA is submitted. This documentation is independently valuable for security architecture review and system documentation.
Reusable risk catalogue:

Completed DPIAs become a risk catalogue for future features. Risks identified and mitigated in one DPIA — prompt injection enabling data exfiltration through tool calls, for example — can be referenced in subsequent DPIAs for similar features, with the mitigation documented as already implemented.
Trade-offs
Template rigidity vs. context:

A prescriptive template can prompt formulaic responses. Assessors who copy-paste standard language for each section without genuinely evaluating the specific feature produce compliant-looking but substantively empty DPIAs. Template design must include guidance that resists this failure mode — for example, requiring specific references to the feature's code or architecture in technical sections.
Maintenance cost:

A template that reflects the current state of agentic AI capabilities and regulatory guidance requires active maintenance. As new agentic risk classes emerge (new tool ecosystems, new orchestration patterns, new regulatory guidance on AI) and as supervisory authority decisions clarify what a DPIA must contain, the template must be updated. Without a designated owner for the template, it gradually becomes outdated.
Scope calibration:

A template designed for complex agentic features imposes unnecessary overhead on simple deployments. A separate lightweight template for lower-risk features prevents the full template from being viewed as disproportionate and bypassed in favour of no documentation at all.
How It Works

The DPIA template is completed sequentially but is best understood in three layers. The first layer is the factual description layer, where assessors document what the feature does, what data it processes, who is involved, and under what legal basis. This layer feeds into the subsequent layers but is itself non-evaluative. The data flow map and processing description sections belong to this layer.

The second layer is the risk evaluation layer, where each identified risk is assessed on two dimensions: likelihood (the probability the risk will materialise) and severity (the magnitude of harm to data subjects if it does). The risk score is the product of these two dimensions, producing a 4x4 matrix where scores range from 1 (Rare x Low) to 16 (Likely x Critical). Scores above a defined threshold (typically 6 or above, corresponding to medium likelihood and medium severity or higher) require DPO review; scores above 9 require proposed mitigation before proceeding to the risk mitigation plan layer.
The risk scoring formula is straightforward:

After mitigation measures are applied, the residual risk score is computed using the same formula with post-mitigation likelihood and severity estimates. The residual score is what the DPO evaluates for acceptability:

The third layer is the mitigation plan layer, where each risk above the acceptable threshold has a concrete mitigation entry specifying the control, its implementation owner, deadline, and verification mechanism. The DPO reviews the mitigation plan as part of the DPIA review; a DPO opinion that the DPIA is adequate requires that the mitigation plan brings all residual risk scores to within the acceptable range.
Architecture

The DPIA document structure for agentic AI combines the five GDPR-mandated sections (left column) with five agentic-specific supplementary sections (right column), producing a unified assessment. The risk mitigation plan at the bottom links each identified risk to its control measure, residual risk score, and DPO acceptability determination. The DPO review outcomes at the bottom govern whether the feature may proceed.

attrs.alt: DPIA document structure showing GDPR Art 35(7) mandatory sections alongside agentic AI supplementary sections and the five-column risk mitigation plan structure

content.text: When to Use It

The full DPIA template applies when the trigger assessment (covered in the preceding sub-subtopic) produces a determination of full DPIA required. The template should be initiated at the point the DPO is notified, before development begins on the feature. The target is to complete all factual description sections before the first engineering sprint commences, so that architectural decisions made during development are informed by the risk assessment.

A lightweight variant of the template applies when the trigger assessment produces a lightweight PIA determination. This variant omits the agentic supplementary sections B (memory analysis) and D (orchestration boundary analysis) where those capabilities are absent from the feature in question. The lightweight template still requires the GDPR mandatory sections and at minimum Section A (agentic data flow map) and Section C (inference and profiling risk).

The template must not be used for retrospective documentation of already-deployed features where no DPIA was conducted. In that scenario, the organisation is in a position of non-compliance. The appropriate response is to commission a retrospective privacy review document rather than a DPIA, since a DPIA is a prospective process and a retrospective document cannot fulfil its privacy-by-design function.
How to Implement

Start by instantiating the DPIA in the organisation's compliance management system, linking it to the development ticket from the trigger assessment. Populate the processing description section first — this requires collaboration between the product manager (for purpose and scope), the engineering lead (for technical architecture and data flows), and the privacy engineer (for data category identification and transfer mapping).

The agentic data flow map (Section A) requires the engineering team to produce a tool call graph — a directed graph where each node is a data source or destination and each edge represents a data access path initiated by the agent. The tool call graph should enumerate every API endpoint the agent may invoke, every data store it reads from or writes to, and every downstream system that receives the agent's outputs. This graph becomes both a DPIA section and a permanent system documentation artefact.

Once the data flows are documented, the risk assessment can be completed. Convene a risk workshop with the privacy engineer, engineering lead, and security architect. Walk through each data flow entry and each agentic multiplier factor, identifying risks at each point. Document each risk with its inherent likelihood and severity scores, then propose and record mitigation measures for each risk above the acceptable threshold.
Examples
Introductory: DPIA Risk Entry Model

The following example implements the DPIA risk entry as a Python dataclass with inherent and residual score computation. This model is the core building block for the risk register section of the DPIA template.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional, List
from datetime import date


class RiskSeverity(Enum):
    LOW = 1
    MEDIUM = 2
    HIGH = 3
    CRITICAL = 4


class RiskLikelihood(Enum):
    RARE = 1
    UNLIKELY = 2
    POSSIBLE = 3
    LIKELY = 4


@dataclass
class DPIARisk:
    risk_id: str
    category: str          # e.g. "inference_profiling", "data_exfiltration"
    description: str
    likelihood: RiskLikelihood
    severity: RiskSeverity
    mitigation: str
    residual_likelihood: RiskLikelihood
    residual_severity: RiskSeverity
    review_trigger: str
    dpo_accepted: bool = False

    @property
    def inherent_score(self) -> int:
        return self.likelihood.value * self.severity.value

    @property
    def residual_score(self) -> int:
        return self.residual_likelihood.value * self.residual_severity.value

    @property
    def risk_reduction_pct(self) -> float:
        if self.inherent_score == 0:
            return 0.0
        return round((1 - self.residual_score / self.inherent_score) * 100, 1)

    def is_acceptable(self, threshold: int = 4) -> bool:
        return self.residual_score <= threshold


# Risk 1: cross-session profiling via persistent vector memory
r1 = DPIARisk(
    risk_id="DPIA-001",
    category="inference_profiling",
    description="Agent retains embeddings across sessions enabling latent profiling of user interests, health patterns, and relationships.",
    likelihood=RiskLikelihood.LIKELY,
    severity=RiskSeverity.HIGH,
    mitigation="Limit embedding retention to 30 days with automatic expiry; encrypt vector store with per-user keys; restrict embedding queries to single-user scope.",
    residual_likelihood=RiskLikelihood.UNLIKELY,
    residual_severity=RiskSeverity.MEDIUM,
    review_trigger="Any extension of retention period or addition of new embedding dimensions.",
)
print("Risk ID:", r1.risk_id)
print("Inherent score:", r1.inherent_score, "(Likely x High =", RiskLikelihood.LIKELY.value, "x", RiskSeverity.HIGH.value, ")")
print("Residual score:", r1.residual_score)
print("Risk reduction:", r1.risk_reduction_pct, "%")
print("Acceptable (threshold=4):", r1.is_acceptable())
Realistic: Full DPIA Document Model

This example models the complete DPIA document structure, including the agentic data flow map, with methods to identify special category flows, cross-border transfers, and to assess whether prior DPA consultation is required under GDPR Article 36.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from datetime import date
from typing import List, Optional
import json


@dataclass
class AgenticDataFlowEntry:
    source_system: str
    data_categories: List[str]
    transfer_mechanism: str       # e.g. "tool_call", "api_response", "memory_retrieval"
    personal_data: bool
    special_category: bool        # GDPR Art 9 special category data
    retention_duration_days: int
    encryption_at_rest: bool
    cross_border_transfer: bool
    third_country: Optional[str] = None  # e.g. "US", "IN" if cross_border_transfer


@dataclass
class DPIADocument:
    dpia_id: str
    feature_name: str
    version: str
    controller_name: str
    processor_names: List[str]
    legal_basis: str              # e.g. "legitimate_interest", "contract", "consent"
    purpose: str
    data_flows: List[AgenticDataFlowEntry]
    risks: List[object]           # List[DPIARisk] - imported from risk module
    dpo_name: str
    dpo_opinion_date: Optional[str] = None
    dpo_accepted: Optional[bool] = None
    dpa_consultation_required: bool = False
    review_date: Optional[str] = None

    @property
    def total_risks(self) -> int:
        return len(self.risks)

    @property
    def unacceptable_risks(self) -> int:
        return sum(1 for r in self.risks if hasattr(r, "is_acceptable") and not r.is_acceptable())

    @property
    def special_category_flows(self) -> List[AgenticDataFlowEntry]:
        return [f for f in self.data_flows if f.special_category]

    @property
    def cross_border_flows(self) -> List[AgenticDataFlowEntry]:
        return [f for f in self.data_flows if f.cross_border_transfer]

    def dpa_consultation_assessment(self) -> bool:
        """Returns True if prior DPA consultation (Art 36 GDPR) is required."""
        return (
            self.unacceptable_risks > 0
            or len(self.special_category_flows) > 0 and self.unacceptable_risks > 0
        )

    def to_json_summary(self) -> str:
        return json.dumps({
            "dpia_id": self.dpia_id,
            "feature_name": self.feature_name,
            "version": self.version,
            "legal_basis": self.legal_basis,
            "data_flow_count": len(self.data_flows),
            "special_category_flow_count": len(self.special_category_flows),
            "cross_border_flow_count": len(self.cross_border_flows),
            "total_risks": self.total_risks,
            "unacceptable_residual_risks": self.unacceptable_risks,
            "dpa_consultation_required": self.dpa_consultation_assessment(),
            "dpo_accepted": self.dpo_accepted,
            "review_date": self.review_date,
        }, indent=2)


# Build a DPIA for an AI research assistant agent
dpia = DPIADocument(
    dpia_id="DPIA-2024-007",
    feature_name="AI Research Assistant with Knowledge Base and Web Search",
    version="1.0",
    controller_name="ExampleOrg Ltd",
    processor_names=["AnthropicCo (LLM)", "PineconeDB (vector store)", "SearchAPIco (web search)"],
    legal_basis="legitimate_interest",
    purpose="Enable employees to research topics using AI-assisted search and knowledge synthesis.",
    data_flows=[
        AgenticDataFlowEntry(
            source_system="User query input",
            data_categories=["query_text"],
            transfer_mechanism="direct_input",
            personal_data=True,
            special_category=False,
            retention_duration_days=30,
            encryption_at_rest=True,
            cross_border_transfer=True,
            third_country="US",
        ),
        AgenticDataFlowEntry(
            source_system="Employee profile store",
            data_categories=["name", "job_role", "department"],
            transfer_mechanism="tool_call",
            personal_data=True,
            special_category=False,
            retention_duration_days=0,
            encryption_at_rest=True,
            cross_border_transfer=False,
        ),
    ],
    risks=[],   # populated from risk module in production
    dpo_name="Jane Smith",
    review_date="2025-07-12",
)
print(dpia.to_json_summary())
Risk Mitigation Plan Builder

The risk mitigation plan tracks each control measure from planning through verification. This example builds a structured plan and renders it as a Markdown table suitable for embedding in the DPIA document.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import List


class MitigationStatus(Enum):
    PLANNED = "planned"
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress"
    IMPLEMENTED = "implemented"
    VERIFIED = "verified"


@dataclass
class RiskMitigationPlanEntry:
    risk_id: str
    mitigation_description: str
    mitigation_type: str          # "technical" or "organisational"
    owner: str
    due_date: str
    status: MitigationStatus
    implementation_evidence: str  # e.g. reference to PR, policy doc, audit report
    verified_by: str
    verification_date: str


def generate_markdown_plan(entries: List[RiskMitigationPlanEntry]) -> str:
    lines = [
        "# Risk Mitigation Plan",
        "",
        "| Risk ID | Mitigation | Type | Owner | Due | Status | Evidence |",
        "| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |",
    ]
    for e in entries:
        lines.append(
            "| {risk_id} | {mitigation} | {mtype} | {owner} | {due} | {status} | {evidence} |".format(
                risk_id=e.risk_id,
                mitigation=e.mitigation_description[:60] + "..." if len(e.mitigation_description) > 60 else e.mitigation_description,
                mtype=e.mitigation_type,
                owner=e.owner,
                due=e.due_date,
                status=e.status.value,
                evidence=e.implementation_evidence,
            )
        )
    return "\n".join(lines)


plan = [
    RiskMitigationPlanEntry(
        risk_id="DPIA-001",
        mitigation_description="Implement 30-day TTL on all vector embeddings with per-user encryption keys",
        mitigation_type="technical",
        owner="platform-eng@example.com",
        due_date="2024-10-15",
        status=MitigationStatus.IMPLEMENTED,
        implementation_evidence="PR #4421, encryption audit report 2024-10-01",
        verified_by="security-review@example.com",
        verification_date="2024-10-20",
    ),
    RiskMitigationPlanEntry(
        risk_id="DPIA-002",
        mitigation_description="Restrict tool call scope to user-owned resources via OAuth 2.0 scoped tokens",
        mitigation_type="technical",
        owner="auth-team@example.com",
        due_date="2024-11-01",
        status=MitigationStatus.IN_PROGRESS,
        implementation_evidence="Design doc ARCH-089",
        verified_by="pending",
        verification_date="pending",
    ),
]
print(generate_markdown_plan(plan))
Real-World Example

A UK financial services firm deploying an AI-powered personal tax assistant completes a DPIA for the feature. The agent accesses HMRC API endpoints, reads the user's bank statement history, and synthesises a summary of taxable income and eligible deductions. The DPIA data flow map identifies six data access points: user query input, HMRC API (tax records), open banking API (bank statements), agent context window (in-memory processing), audit log store, and user notification channel.

The risk register identifies four risks above the acceptable threshold. Risk DPIA-001 (cross-session profiling from financial data embeddings) receives an inherent score of 12 (Likely x Critical); the proposed mitigation of session-scoped context only (no persistent vector store) reduces this to 3 (Unlikely x Low). Risk DPIA-002 (inference of political views from charitable donation patterns) receives an inherent score of 9; the mitigation of filtering inference outputs against a prohibited-inference blocklist reduces residual risk to 4. Risk DPIA-003 (data exfiltration via prompt injection in HMRC API responses) receives inherent score 8; the mitigation of strict output schema validation and tool output sanitisation reduces residual to 2. Risk DPIA-004 (unauthorised access to third-party financial data) receives inherent score 12; mitigated by per-request OAuth 2.0 token scoping to residual 4.

All residual risk scores fall to 4 or below (the firm's acceptable threshold is 4 for features processing special category data). The DPO accepts the DPIA with one condition: the prohibited-inference blocklist for Risk DPIA-002 must be reviewed and extended quarterly by the DPO, rather than annually as initially proposed. The mitigation plan is updated, and development proceeds.
Common Mistakes
Risk Matrix Without Evidence

A risk register that assigns likelihood and severity scores without documented rationale for each score is a compliance facade. DPOs reviewing such assessments cannot determine whether the scores reflect actual analysis or optimistic guessing. Every risk entry must include explicit rationale for its likelihood score (why is this risk merely unlikely rather than possible, given the feature's data access scope?) and for its severity score (why is the harm low rather than high, given the nature of the data?). Without rationale, the DPO must either trust the scores or re-examine the underlying evidence themselves.
Mitigation Plan Without Owners and Deadlines

Risk mitigation entries that specify a control measure without assigning an owner and deadline are not mitigation plans — they are wish lists. If Risk DPIA-001 is mitigated by a 30-day embedding TTL but no engineer is assigned to implement it and no sprint deadline is set, the DPIA contains an unimplemented control that the organisation is relying on for compliance. The DPO must reject DPIAs where mitigation plan entries lack specific owners and implementation deadlines.
Omitting Inferred Data from the Data Flow Map

The agentic data flow map section is frequently completed as a list of input data sources without considering what data the agent generates internally. An agent that infers from calendar and communication patterns that a user is likely to be pregnant, or that a spending pattern suggests the user is experiencing financial stress, has produced new personal data — potentially special category data under GDPR Article 9 — even if that inference is never stored explicitly. The data flow map must include a column for agent-generated inferences, and the risk register must evaluate the privacy implications of those inferences.
Best Practices
Use a risk workshop format:

Rather than having the privacy engineer complete the DPIA alone, convene a structured workshop with engineering, legal, and product present. The risk identification step benefits from multiple perspectives; engineers identify technical vulnerabilities that legal assessors would miss, and legal assessors identify regulatory implications that engineers may not consider.
Link the data flow map to the threat model:

The DPIA's agentic data flow map and the security threat model (STRIDE or equivalent) should share the same tool call graph. Maintaining two separate graphs increases documentation overhead and creates divergence over time; a single authoritative graph that feeds both processes is more maintainable.
Set residual risk thresholds by data sensitivity:

Use a lower residual risk acceptance threshold (e.g. 3) for features processing special category data or data belonging to vulnerable populations, and a higher threshold (e.g. 6) for features processing routine employee data. A single universal threshold treats all data equally, which underprotects sensitive data and over-restricts routine processing.
Treat the mitigation plan as a sprint artifact:

Each mitigation plan entry with an implementation deadline should map to a development ticket in the sprint backlog. The DPIA is not complete from an operational standpoint until all mitigation entries with deadlines in the current release cycle are in the sprint and assigned to engineers.
Establish a DPIA review cadence:

Set a maximum validity period for each DPIA (typically 12 months or until the next significant change, whichever comes first). The review date should appear in the DPIA header, and the compliance system should automatically notify the DPO and the feature team when a DPIA is approaching expiry.
Performance and Scalability

At low feature velocity, the DPIA process creates manageable overhead: one or two DPIAs per month can be completed and reviewed within a two-week window, fitting within normal sprint cycles. At higher velocity — more than five concurrent DPIAs — the DPO review bottleneck becomes significant. Organisations can address this by training privacy champions in each engineering team to complete the factual and risk sections to a higher quality standard, reserving DPO time for the sign-off rather than the assessment phase.

The agentic data flow map section has a specific scalability challenge: agents with dynamically configurable tool access (where users can add or remove tool integrations at runtime) cannot be assessed with a static tool call graph. The DPIA for such platforms must assess the maximum possible tool access scope — the worst case — rather than the minimum currently deployed, because the privacy controls must hold under the maximum scope.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Compliance management platforms such as OneTrust, TrustArc, Osano, and DataGrail provide DPIA workflow modules with pre-built templates. These are calibrated for conventional data processing and require customisation to add the five agentic supplementary sections. The ICO (UK Information Commissioner's Office) and the CNIL (French DPA) publish DPIA templates that reflect their respective national guidance; these can be adapted as the base for the GDPR mandatory sections.

For organisations using Confluence or Notion as documentation platforms, the DPIA template can be implemented as a page template with required sections enforced through mandatory field prompts. Jira integration via automation rules can create DPIA tickets automatically when the trigger assessment determines a full DPIA is required, pre-populating the DPIA template with the feature metadata from the trigger assessment record.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Description
Strengths
Weaknesses
Structured DPIA template (this approach)
Prescriptive sections with evidence prompts for both GDPR mandatory and agentic-specific risks
Consistent, auditable, forces data enumeration; covers agentic risks explicitly
Risk of formulaic completion; requires maintenance as guidance evolves
Free-form narrative DPIA
Assessor writes a bespoke document covering Art 35(7) elements in narrative form
Flexible; can address novel risks better than a template's predefined sections
Inconsistent completeness; DPO review is slower; easy to omit mandatory elements inadvertently
Automated DPIA tool
SaaS platform generates DPIA from questionnaire answers and data inventory scans
Fast initial completion; auto-populates known data categories from existing RoPA
Poor coverage of agentic AI risk classes; tool-generated text often lacks the evidential depth DPOs need
Regulatory authority template
Use the ICO, CNIL, or other DPA's published DPIA template directly
Strong regulatory alignment; DPA-reviewable if consultation required under Art 36
Generic; does not address agentic AI risk classes; may not be jurisdiction-appropriate for all processing
Security and Reliability

The DPIA document itself is a sensitive record. It contains a detailed enumeration of the organisation's data processing activities, identified vulnerabilities, and their mitigations — a profile that would be valuable to an attacker scoping a data theft, a regulator conducting an inspection, or a litigant in a data protection enforcement action. DPIAs must be stored with access controls that restrict read access to: the DPO, the privacy engineer assigned to the feature, legal counsel, and the engineering lead. The mitigation plan entries referencing unimplemented controls are particularly sensitive, as they document known vulnerabilities before they are addressed.

Reliability of the DPIA process requires ensuring that the mitigation plan entries are actually implemented and verified before launch, not just documented. Organisations that produce thorough DPIAs but launch features before the mitigation plan is complete have achieved compliance theatre rather than privacy protection. The process must include a pre-launch gate that verifies each mitigation plan entry is marked as implemented with evidence before the feature goes to production.
Mental Model

Think of the DPIA template as a structural engineer's checklist for a building. The checklist does not design the building — the architects and engineers do that. But it ensures that every mandatory safety check has been performed (load calculations, fire egress, seismic resistance) before the building is certified for occupancy. A building that looks sound but where the structural engineer skipped the load calculation is a building that will eventually collapse.

The agentic AI supplementary sections are the equivalent of the flood risk assessment or the wind tunnel test for unusually tall structures — additional checks that are not required for every building but are mandatory when the structural characteristics create elevated risk. An agent with persistent memory and broad tool access is the equivalent of the unusually tall structure: the standard checklist is necessary but not sufficient.
Key Takeaways

GDPR Article 35(7) mandates four elements in every DPIA: processing description, necessity and proportionality assessment, risk assessment, and risk mitigation measures; a DPIA template operationalises these elements into answerable sections rather than leaving assessors to interpret the legislation directly.

Agentic AI DPIAs require five supplementary sections beyond the GDPR minimum: agentic data flow map, memory and context analysis, inference and profiling risk assessment, orchestration boundary analysis, and model supplier assessment.

Risk scoring uses a likelihood times severity matrix producing inherent and residual scores; the residual score (after mitigation) is what the DPO evaluates for acceptability, and DPA consultation under Article 36 is required when residual scores remain above the acceptable threshold.
The risk mitigation plan is not complete unless each entry specifies an owner, an implementation deadline, and a verification mechanism; entries without these fields are not mitigation commitments.

The agentic data flow map must include agent-generated inferences as a data category, not just input data sources; inferences about special category attributes derived from behaviour patterns are personal data even if never explicitly stored.

A pre-launch gate that verifies all mitigation plan entries are marked as implemented with evidence is essential; organisations that launch features before mitigation plan completion achieve documentation compliance but not privacy protection.

DPIAs should have a maximum validity period (typically 12 months) with automatic renewal notifications; features that evolve significantly after the initial DPIA require reassessment rather than reliance on a stale document.
